cases.title: Breathing Stopped, Pulse Present
cases.presentation: An adult is found unresponsive after a sedative overdose. He is not breathing but has a strong, regular carotid pulse of about 70/min. The scene is safe and help has been called.
cases.question: What is the correct BLS intervention?
options: B: Provide breaths at 1 every 6 seconds and reassess the pulse every 2 minutes.
C: Apply an AED and shock.
D: Place him in the recovery position and wait.
cases.explanation: This is respiratory arrest with a pulse. The correct action is rescue breathing at 1 breath every 6 seconds for an adult, reassessing the pulse about every 2 minutes and starting CPR if the pulse is lost. Compressions are not indicated while a pulse is present.
cases.clinicalPearl: A pulse with no breathing means breaths only — and watch that pulse, because respiratory arrest can slide into cardiac arrest.

cases.title: Child Not Breathing
cases.presentation: A 4-year-old is pulled from a backyard pond. She is unresponsive and not breathing, but you feel a definite pulse of about 90/min.
cases.question: What rescue-breathing rate should you use?
cases.explanation: Children and infants with a pulse but inadequate breathing receive breaths at the faster pediatric rate of 1 every 2–3 seconds. Because drowning is a breathing-driven emergency, prompt ventilation is especially important here.
cases.clinicalPearl: Kids breathe faster than adults — rescue-breathe them faster too: one breath every 2–3 seconds.

cases.title: Deterioration During Rescue Breathing
cases.presentation: You have been providing rescue breaths to an adult in respiratory arrest for 2 minutes. On your scheduled pulse reassessment, you can no longer feel a carotid pulse within 10 seconds.
cases.question: What should you do now?
options: B: Begin CPR immediately, starting with chest compressions.
C: Reassess the pulse for another 30 seconds.
cases.explanation: Loss of a pulse means the respiratory arrest has progressed to cardiac arrest. Start CPR immediately, beginning with compressions, and apply an AED as soon as available.
cases.clinicalPearl: The 2-minute pulse check exists precisely to catch this moment — respiratory arrest becoming cardiac arrest.
